description:
You are an integration-developer validating that two offline mock API responses contain the expected control-fields and that their values can be extracted for a quick QA report.

    1. Starting situation  
       • A directory already exists: /home/user/api_samples  
       • Inside it are exactly two files containing JSON objects (one per file):
         – /home/user/api_samples/response1.json  
         – /home/user/api_samples/response2.json  

       response1.json contains only this object (no extra spaces or trailing commas):
         {
           "id": 101,
           "status": "success",
           "elapsed_ms": 123,
           "payload": {
             "result": "OK"
           }
         }

       response2.json contains only this object:
         {
           "id": 102,
           "status": "error",
           "elapsed_ms": 456,
           "payload": {
             "result": "FAIL"
           }
         }

    2. Your goal  
       Produce two new text files in /home/user/api_samples exactly as specified below.  
       • /home/user/api_samples/summary.csv – a comma-separated file consolidating the three control fields from both responses.  
       • /home/user/api_samples/process.log – a plain-text log indicating that each input file was processed.

    3. Exact required content

       3.1 summary.csv  
       • It must have exactly three columns, in this order: id, status, elapsed_ms  
       • The header row must be present as the first line.  
       • Each subsequent line must contain the values extracted from the JSON files, sorted by id in ascending numeric order.  
       • Every line—including the last—must end with a single UNIX newline (LF, “\n”).  
       • The file must contain exactly these three lines:

         id,status,elapsed_ms
         101,success,123
         102,error,456

       3.2 process.log  
       • One line per processed file, in the order the files were handled.  
       • The required literal text for each line is:  
           response1.json processed  
           response2.json processed  
       • No extra spaces, tabs or timestamps.  
       • Terminate each line with a single LF and ensure the file ends with a newline.  
       • The file must therefore contain exactly two lines:

         response1.json processed
         response2.json processed

    4. Constraints  
       • Complete the task with at most 3–4 shell commands (pipelines are fine).  
       • Do NOT modify the original JSON files.  
       • Do NOT create additional files or directories.  
       • Do NOT leave temporary/intermediate files behind (the test harness fails if unexpected files appear).

    5. Verification  
       The automated tests will:  
       • Confirm that /home/user/api_samples/summary.csv and /home/user/api_samples/process.log exist.  
       • Byte-for-byte compare each file against the exact content listed above.  

    6. What you should return to the user  
       Only the terminal commands you executed and any relevant console output that naturally appears (e.g., from cat).  
       Do not embed any commentary outside normal shell comments.
truth:
Initial state (already present before the agent starts):

    Directory:
      /home/user/api_samples    (owner: user, mode 755)

    Files and contents:

    /home/user/api_samples/response1.json
    -------------------------------------
    {
      "id": 101,
      "status": "success",
      "elapsed_ms": 123,
      "payload": {
        "result": "OK"
      }
    }

    /home/user/api_samples/response2.json
    -------------------------------------
    {
      "id": 102,
      "status": "error",
      "elapsed_ms": 456,
      "payload": {
        "result": "FAIL"
      }
    }

    Expected files after successful task completion:

    /home/user/api_samples/summary.csv
    ----------------------------------
    id,status,elapsed_ms
    101,success,123
    102,error,456

    /home/user/api_samples/process.log
    ----------------------------------
    response1.json processed
    response2.json processed
